speaker: interviewer
text: --more questions. First of all, what are your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marshall Howard Winner-- no-- Marshall Benjamin Winner. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one is Joel Howard Winner. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there are great grandchildren-- Jake, who is adopted, and Zacharie Cole Winner.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are presently retired with your husband in Phoenix.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before I ask you my last ending questions, is there anything you feel you've left out in your testimony that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I-- what happened to some of the friends-- for instance, the cantor died in the Warsaw uprising. He was shipped to Warsaw, and he died there. He was in the uprising. And many other friends just didn't get out that I don't know anything about. All I know is the-- life has been good. And when you think about the past, it's-- maybe we have grown because of it-- personally. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My belief in human beings is very strong. Not all human beings are bad. My belief in family is very strong. I believe in being together and supporting each other morally, physically, mentally. I believe in Judaism very strong. At times you forget to believe in God, but you never forget-- I never forget to believe in Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are the-- what-- what you feel got you through and helped you to survive-- these fundamental beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to-- not lately anymore. Yeah. It's been a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we were together, yeah-- that they weren't dead-- all these friends, all this family. And why was I chosen to survive? This is an un-ever great question. Of all the people that I've known that didn't survive, there are only few who did. Why were we chosen? Must be an answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, you can say-- to tell the others that are coming after us. But that's only-- that only comes afterwards. It still doesn't answer, personally-- why me? Yeah. What advice would you have for your children, your grandchildren, your great grandchildren, and future generations, after having lived through what you did? What advice would you like to impart to them for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't tell me you were going to ask that. [LAUGHS] First and foremost, family-- to be a family, to be supportive of each other. I think that's one of the more important things; to believe that you are strong, that you can help, if necessary, and not to shy away from it, not to say, let the next one do it. You've got to do it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Believe in God if you can. It's difficult to do that. But then, of course, we were spared. Why were we spared? This is the-- the question that's been in my mind ever since the Holocaust came to being. Must be a reason. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Abraham Wexler, who was one of the ancestors. My grandmother on my mother's side was a born Wexler. The book is-- the book he's holding under his arm is a Haggadah, and the picture you see-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --oh, for-- the picture you see is the 10 plagues. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have-- it's done in-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just stop. Just go ahead and-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Abraham Wexler, one of the ancestors. My grandmother on my mother's side was a born Wexler. The picture goes back to 1793. He lived for about 50 years. The book he's holding is beside the picture, and it was a Haggadah-- is still is a Haggadah. And it shows the 10 plagues on the picture there. It's all handwritten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some place in Germany where they lived-- long time ago. It's painted in pastels. That's why it has a glass over it. It's in the original frame with the original glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came through our family, and it was one of the things that we were able to bring. We-- this is one of the first things we put in the trunk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rabbi Loeb Chaim Wexler. He lived from 1823 to 1900. He was the son of Rabbi Abraham Wexler. He started the yeshiva in Schwabach, Germany, and he's buried in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this-- so what relative is this of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a great, great, great, great grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are the parents of Asur Benima, on their golden wedding anniversary. This was taken circa 1895. Asur Benima is my mother's father, so these are grandparents of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the woman's name in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have her name. I don't have his name either, only that it was Benima. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Asur Benima. He lived from 1852 to 1927. He is the husband of Caroline Wexler, father of Klara Houeslich, father of Abraham Benima. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is Klara Benima. She was married to Asur Benima-- wrong name. She was my mother's mother. She was born in Schwabach, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1900. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Julius Houeslich and Klara Houeslich. Her maiden name was Benima. They are my parents. Julius was born in the Rhineland, in Bedburg, and Klara was born in Furth.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died in 1961, on his 71st birthday. And my mother died at age 84, in 1974, in Baltimore-- both of them in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ruth and Liselott Houeslich. Liselott was born on the 3rd of November, 1915, and Ruth on the 24th of February, 1920. This is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you? On the left or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the left. I'm the little one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister is where today?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is in Herzliya, Israel. She's lived there all her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Furth. These are probably party dresses. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Julius and Klara Houeslich on the left, and Aunt Bertha Benima on the right. She was married to my mother's brother. She was instrumental in getting affidavits for us to come to America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Julius Houeslich in very happy times in Germany, in the very early 1920s-- my father. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is Machen Houeslich and Louise Houeslich. Her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were married 1918-- 63. They were married 50 years. They were shipped to Theresienstadt and died in a cattle car on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: On their golden wedding anniversary in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedburg. And the whole town was bedecked in flags and garlands. And everybody celebrated-- all 5,000 people. And then they shipped them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the three Houeslich brothers-- Heinrich, Joseph, and Julius. Joseph was killed at Verdun, in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your father is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right. And Heinrich went to Argentina and died there at a very ripe age, 92. These are some of the cars that were confiscated by the Nazis from my father's business, and which he didn't get paid for. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is George Rau, who was arrested and died in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he in relationship to?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was my friend, and we were going to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he gave it to me. Yeah. This was my mother when she was 17 years old. She was very beautiful. No wonder my father fell in love with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire Benima Houeslich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Furth. This is Julius Houeslich and his daughter, Ruth Houeslich, and their dog, Rocky, who also played a role. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Cantor Benjamin Freund. He was the cantor in the [NON-ENGLISH] before it was torn down by the Nazis. He had a beautiful baritone voice, and he died in the Warsaw ghetto uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew him personal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ruth Houeslich the day she got married to William Oppenheimer, on the 1st of September, 1940. By the time we're finished, I'm learning all this. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is Joel Howard Winner. He was born in 1970, and he's our second grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- in Fort Drum, New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth would you introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd be delighted to. This is Willie, my husband, William, of 55 years. And next to me on the right is my daughter Shirley Foutch. And in front of her is her husband, William Foutch. Right in front of me is one of my grandsons, Marshall Benjamin Winner. Unfortunately, the other one, Joel Howard Winner, is not present. Next to Marshall is his lovely wife, Kim. And in front of Marshall is Jake, who is having a birthday. And I'm holding Zacharie Cole Winner, our great grandson, like Jay-- Jake. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have-- drew a blank. Would anybody like to say something to Ruth now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm kind of probably the newest one-- other than Zacharie-- the newest member of the family. But just being-- getting familiar with all that's going on-- just glad that the people that made it through had the fortitude and strength to be here to continue this, or he wouldn't be here. He wouldn't be here, you know. I would have been very unhappy had she not been the way she was, and her family been the way she was to bring her here in the first place. So I have a lot to thank her for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think the-- the most that I have to say is to thank both my parents for coming. My father came a lot sooner. My mother came in '40. And thank God they met and had the desire to want to have a child. And I guess the only way that I can say it is I was a chosen child. I'm adopted, and I've known it all my life. And I have never wanted to know anything about my background, I-- because of the love, and the upbringing, and the education that I got. Because I-- these are my parents. Blood doesn't necessarily make you a parent, although I have to thank my mom and my dad wherever they are, whoever they are, for giving birth to me and having the fortitude for whatever reason they gave me up. Then I think that they did because they felt that they couldn't have given me what they needed to. And if my mom weren't here and I wasn't here, then I wouldn't have a family. And I have an absolutely marvelous family. I have two of the most beautiful grandsons that I could ever have, and I bless-- every-- I-- I bless my life for my sons. And the one that isn't with us is in Kentucky, at Fort Campbell. He's in the service. But for my-- my first child to have picked such a marvelous woman to make a part of his life, and for her to have such a marvelous son, so that I have an eight-year-old grandson and then we have a brand new little one who's six months old. So that's very special. I think it's been a very special life for all of us and my husband, who is now part of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We adore him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He's special.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I want to say, as-- as Kim, I'm also fairly new to the family. But I'd just like to thank my mother and father-in-law for what they have done for not only me, but for my family-- my wife and I. And I-- and I am glad that they are here, because if-- as-- as with Kim, I would not be here either. And they-- both of them are very special to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Is there anyone else who would like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jake, you wanted to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're just glad you're all here.
